data.title: Inditex: Cyclical Slowdown Not Likely To Reverse Soon
data.description:
As I’ve covered in a previous article, while I see Industria de Diseno Textil – Inditex (OTCPK:IDEXY) as an interesting growth play in the retail sector over the long term, its full valuation and moderating growth were two major concerns some months ago and therefore my recommendation on its shares was ‘hold’ at the time. Since then, its shares have traded sideways but have beaten slightly the market during the same period, showing that upside potential was indeed limited, at least in the short term.

A couple of months ago, the company released its Fiscal Year (FY) 2024 earnings, reporting slower sales growth and earnings slightly below expectations, justifying its relatively weak share price momentum in recent months.

Indeed, Inditex’s revenues amounted to €38.6 billion in the last fiscal year, up by about 7.5% compared to the previous one. While this is still an acceptable growth rate, it’s much lower than compared to its strong operating momentum following the pandemic and has been on a declining trend in recent quarters, being concerning for a ‘growth’ company like Inditex. This slower growth was also penalized by currency moves, which had an impact of about three percentage points on sales growth, which is quite significant considering that about 65% of the company’s sales are generated in Europe.

By brand, all segments reported higher revenues during the last fiscal year, but its flagship brand Zara reported sales growth of 6.6% YoY, being an underperformer within the group, while brands like Stradivarius and Massimo Dutti had better performances with annual sales growth above 20%.

After the end of FY 2024, Inditex has launched the spring/summer collection and sales growth between the beginning of February to March 10 th was only 4%, showing that the deceleration of sales growth has not reversed in the first weeks of FY 2025, raising concerns about the company’s growth outlook in the short term. This is the weakest growth since the pandemic and suggests a cyclical slowdown justified by external issues, namely weak economic growth across Europe and some weakness in Asia and the Americas, rather than, in my opinion, some fundamental issues about its business model.

This also reflects some growth normalization following a strong growth period following the pandemic, which was not sustainable, and Inditex is now returning to a more ‘normal’ sales growth path of 6-8% on an annual basis. Indeed, Inditex’s long-term guidance is to achieve 4-6% like-for-like sales growth, plus add about 2% sales growth from new space, which means its sales growth in FY 2024 was not much different than what the company expects to achieve over the long term.

Beyond sales growth, other important financial metrics reported strong figures in FY 2024, showing that Inditex is doing a good job to improving operating efficiency and protecting its business margins. Showing that despite slowing sales growth its pricing has remained robust, Inditex’s gross margin was 57.8% in FY 2024, representing an improvement of 8 basis points (bps) compared to the previous year. Regarding operating expenses, the company also did a good job to control cost growth, leading to overall expenses of €11.5 billion (+6.5% YoY), which increased less than sales growth during the past fiscal year. This means the company was able to slightly improve its operating leverage, reaching a new record operating profit margin of 19.6%, ten basis points above its previous record high achieved in FY 2013.

Despite this positive operating performance, there were some factors suggesting that recent growth slowdown is not likely to reverse soon, namely inventories increased by 12% YoY to more than €3.3 billion at the end of FY 2024, representing more than 31 days of sales. While this is a small difference compared to the end of the previous FY, higher inventories is not a good signal about consumer demand for its products, especially during a period in which Inditex is making significant investments in logistics.

Indeed, due to strong sales growth over the past few years, Inditex decided to make a large investment on logistics in Spain and the Netherlands, as the company was quite confident about consumer demand for its brands over the medium to long term. This is putting some pressure on the company’s free cash flow generation given that capital expenditures amounted to more than €2.6 billion in FY 2024, up by 42% YoY.

Because of this larger investment in logistics, its free cash flow declined over the last year despite the company reaching record earnings, but the decline was only 5% from the previous year to €4.8 billion. This was a good outcome considering the significant capex increase, a trend that is expected to be maintained during 2025, as Inditex’s investments on logistics should be spread between the two fiscal years. Indeed, according to analysts’ estimates, Inditex’s capex is expected to be about €2.7 billion in FY 2025, but decline to a more ‘normal’ level of about €2 billion in FY 2026, while free cash flow should return to a growing path thereafter.

Regarding the company’s balance sheet position, it remained very strong as Inditex had a net cash position of €11.5 billion at the end of last fiscal year and practically don’t have any financial debt. This net cash position represents close to 8% of its current market value of about €148 billion, showing that Inditex’s cash position is quite large, and its balance sheet is clearly strong.

This strong balance sheet and the company’s high profitability levels are clearly supportive of a growing dividend over the next few years, even though Inditex is somewhat conservative and is only targeting a dividend payout ratio of 60%, plus some bonus dividend payments. This seems somewhat low for a company that has very strong fundamentals and does not need to retain much cash, but Inditex’s ordinary dividend growth is likely to remain similar to earnings growth ahead, while special dividends are likely in the future as the company does not need to retain earnings due to a strong balance sheet position.

Related to FY 2024 earnings, Inditex increased its dividend by 9% YoY to €1.68 per share including both ordinary and special dividends, a growth rate that was quite close to earnings growth in the year. At its current share price leads to a dividend yield of about 3.5%. This is an interesting yield for a growth company like Inditex and is above-average compared to other companies in the apparel industry, showing that its profile is switching from growth to a more mature company and, potentially, income may become an important factor of its investment case over the coming years.

Having said that, I think most investors still see Inditex as a growth play and the company’s guidance is to grow gross store space at about 5% per year during 2025-26, being an import support for the company to reach sales growth in the high-single digits over the coming years. On the other hand, the trade war and new tariffs in the U.S. may be a headwind for sales growth in the short term, even though Inditex’s exposure to the U.S. is not much large, but it can be an important setback for higher sales growth ahead.

Regarding its valuation, its shares have de-rated somewhat in recent months as business growth has moderated and investors have increasingly become more concerned about Inditex’s growth prospects in the short term. Indeed, Inditex is currently trading at about 23x forward earnings, slightly cheaper than compared to when I last covered it (24.4x forward earnings at the time). This multiple is now closer to its historical average over the past five years, but investors should be aware that Inditex usually trades between 20-25x forward earnings. Thus I still don’t see its shares as being undervalued right now.

Conclusion

Inditex has strong fundamentals and positive growth prospects over the long term, but it’s currently suffering a cyclical slowdown that doesn’t seem likely to reverse soon. Given that economic uncertainty has increased in recent weeks due to the U.S. trade war, I think long-term investors should wait for a pullback to enter into a position, or buy more shares, as earnings revisions seem to be biased to the downside ahead which could lead to a lower share price in the near term.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Kimbell Royalty Partners: Reduced Potential Dilution After Partial Redemption Of Preferred Units
data.description:
Kimbell Royalty Partners (NYSE:KRP) redeemed half of its Series A Preferred Units in early May. This slightly reduces its near-term distributable cash flow, but saves it money (or the potential dilution from conversion) in the long-term.

Kimbell's credit facility utilization is near 75% after redeeming those preferred units, which is why it didn't redeem all of its preferred units. It should be able to slowly work down its credit facility debt while keeping its payout ratio at 75%.

Kimbell's Q1 2025 results were solid, as it reached a new high in production due to its Mabee Ranch acquisition. Kimbell declared a distribution of $0.47 per unit in Q1 2025.

Kimbell also reaffirmed its full-year guidance, although I'd expect its quarterly distribution to average around $0.36 per unit during the remainder of the year now. This is due to lower oil prices, with Kimbell having swaps covering close to 20% of its production.

I've reduced my long-term oil price estimate to $70 from $75 now, which is the main contributor to the change in Kimbell's estimated value to $16.75 per unit from $18.50 per unit.

Series A Preferred Unit Redemption

Kimbell redeemed half of its outstanding Series A Cumulative Convertible Preferred Units on May 7, 2025. It redeemed 162,500 Series A Preferred Units (with an issue price of $1,000 per unit) for $182.3 million. This was a redemption price of $1,121.92 per unit.

This results in a slight near-term hit (around $4 million) to Kimbell's distributable cash flow due to the redemption premium and the slightly higher interest rate on its credit facility debt.

At the end of Q1 2025, Kimbell's credit facility debt had a 7.68% interest rate versus the 6% distribution rate on the Series A Preferred Units.

Due to the minimum IRR requirement in the redemption price calculation, the cost to redeem the Series A Preferred Units will keep going up. Thus it makes sense for Kimbell to redeem the units now despite the slight negative near-term impact on distributable cash flow.

Kimbell's credit facility borrowing base (and aggregate commitments) were both increased from $550 million to $625 million with the spring redetermination. After giving effect to the Series A Preferred Unit redemption and the debt paydown from 25% of its Q1 2025 distributable cash flow, Kimbell expects to have around $462 million in credit facility debt.

Kimbell likely would have redeemed more of its Series A Preferred Units if it had more room under its credit facility, but the redemption of half of those units brought its credit facility utilization up to around 74%. Kimbell will now be working down its credit facility debt for a while.

Q1 2025 Results

Kimbell's Q1 2025 production was solid, with 25,501 BOEPD in average production during the quarter. This includes 74 days of production from Kimbell's Mabee Ranch acquisition.

Including the pre-closing production from this deal (with Kimbell receiving the related revenues anyway), Kimbell's Q1 2025 production would have been 25,841 BOEPD. This is slightly above its full-year production guidance midpoint of 25,500 BOEPD.

Kimbell noted it had 90 rigs drilling on its acreage at the end of Q1 2025, down 1 rig from the end of 2024 (including the Mabee Ranch assets). Kimbell's market share remained at 16% of all Continental US land rigs.

The most recent rig count report indicates a roughly 2% decline in Continental US land rigs, so I'd expect around 88 rigs to be drilling on Kimbell's acreage currently.

Updated 2025 Outlook

Kimbell reaffirmed its guidance for around 25,500 BOEPD (33% oil) in 2025 production.

At current strip prices of $63 to $64 WTI oil and $3.90 NYMEX natural gas, Kimbell is now projected to generate $329 million in 2025 revenues after hedges.

Kimbell's hedges are positive value for oil and slightly negative value for natural gas, resulting in an estimated $2 million in positive value for 2025 currently.

Kimbell is now projected to generate $222 million in 2025 distributable cash flow at current strip. This is around $2.06 per unit in distributable cash flow for the full year.

Kimbell has a 75% payout ratio, so it may put $56 million towards debt reduction and $166 million ($1.54 per unit) towards common unit distributions for the year.

Kimbell's Q1 2025 distribution was $0.47 per unit, but its distribution may average a lower amount (such as $0.36 per unit) during the remaining quarters in 2025. This would be due to lower oil prices, as well as a modest impact from the preferred unit redemptions as mentioned above.

Estimated Valuation

Kimbell's Q1 2025 performance was solid, and it reaffirmed its full-year guidance. However, I've tweaked its estimated value downwards from $18.50 per unit down to $16.75 per unit.

This new estimated value is based on long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas. The majority of the change is due to my long-term oil price dropping from $75 to $70. This reduces Kimbell's annual distributable free cash flow by around $0.16 per unit.

As well, there is some impact from lower near-term oil prices in 2025 and 2026 based on strip.

Conclusion

Kimbell redeemed half of its $325 million in Series A Preferred Units in early May. This reduces the potential future dilution from the conversion of the preferred units into common units. The move does result in around a $4 million per year near-term reduction in Kimbell's distributable cash flow, but those units get more expensive to redeem as time goes on.

I now expect Kimbell's quarterly distribution to average around $0.36 per unit during the last three quarters of 2025 based on current strip prices. I have also revised its estimated value to $16.75 per unit based on long-term $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

data.title: SR Bancorp Is Likely To Cool Off This Summer
data.description:
The last couple of years have been a bit of a whirlwind for SR Bancorp (NASDAQ:SRBK). After existing under a number of different names since its beginnings in the late 1800s, the company reorganized in 2023 and completed an IPO valued at just over $90 million. As part of the conversion into a new, publicly traded company, the company also acquired Regal Bancorp, adding three members of Regal's board of directors to its own governing body. When these transactions were complete, SR Bancorp had established a modest footprint in Northern New Jersey, with 14 bank branches and one loan office.

Despite all of this recent activity, the bank operates in virtual anonymity. No Wall Street analysts follow SRBK, and the company has not even been covered by a writer from Seeking Alpha since its IPO. I decided to look into the bank’s operations to see if it could be a diamond in the rough. The stock is up an amazing 43.5% in the last 12 months, but my research tells me that type of price appreciation is unlikely to happen again unless some big changes happen for the company. At best, I believe the stock is worthy of only a hold recommendation for the reasons that I will cover in this article.

Company Overview

With its corporate offices in Bound Brook, NJ, about 25 miles from Newark, SR Bancorp is the holding company for Somerset Regal Bank. SRBK has a market cap of just under $120 million, but somehow the bank felt even smaller when I tried to research its financial information from quarterly and annual reports. The Investor Relations page on the company website has not been updated with news since September 20, 2023. The company lists the name of the CEO, William Taylor, as the only option on its Investor Contacts page, with his phone number and email address.

For the last 12 months, total revenues for the bank were $28 million, but net income was in the red with a loss of about $100,000. The losses can be attributed, in part, to costs from the merger and IPO. At the end of its last quarter, SR Bancorp had just over $835.6 million in deposits and a net loan balance of $780.8 million. Those loans are primarily commercial and residential real estate loans. A snapshot of the company’s loan portfolio from its most recent 10-Q released on May 15 is given below.

If considered by their share of net loans from the bank, residential mortgages compose 53.0% of the portfolio. Commercial loans, which include multifamily loans, constitute most of the balance at 45.5%. The bank states that “nearly all” of its loans are servicing properties within the bank’s primary market area in northern New Jersey.

The stock pays a quarterly dividend of $0.05, giving SRBK a yield of 1.53%. The company has reported $0.34 of earnings in the first three quarters of the current fiscal year, so the dividend appears to be relatively safe at this time.

Analysis of SR Bancorp

Comparisons to financial metrics that go back to the company’s first fiscal year are challenging because of several charges and expenses related to the merger that were non-recurring. The company's fiscal year ends June 30, so for most of my research, I looked at the data from the second half of 2024 (Q1 and Q2) and the first quarter of 2025 (Q3). Unfortunately, all the reported performance ratios for the bank appear to be moving in the wrong direction. Below is a list of data points taken from SRBK’s most recent quarterly reports.

ROA, ROE, and NIM are all getting gradually worse, and the company's earnings per share are following suit. The net interest margin places SRBK slightly below the national average for banks of its size, according to a recent report from the FDIC. The national average for a bank like SR Bancorp with just over $1 billion in total assets is approximately 3.4%.

Margins are a challenge for SR Bancorp because the bank has a high number of interest-bearing accounts, relative to its total deposit base. Out of the $835.6 million in deposits reported at the end of March this year, the company is paying interest on 87.3% of that total, leaving only 12.7% as non-interest-bearing. This is an improvement from Q2 when this number was only 11.5%, but it is still lower than most of its peers.

The percentage of non-interest-bearing deposits at three other banks with similar size and operations are given below. While the comparisons are not exactly apples to apples, each bank selected has a market cap within $10 million of SRBK. Each also has a noticeably higher share of non-interest-bearing accounts.

  • Riverview Bancorp (RVSB) – 25.6%
  • South Atlantic Bancshares (OTCQX:SABK) – 20.8%
  • SB Financial Group (SBFG) – 18.9%

One bright spot I could find for SRBK from the end of its last quarter was a significant increase in non-interest income. Compared to the same time period in the previous fiscal year, this revenue source has increased 41%, or $571,000, to $2.0 million for the nine months ended March 31, 2025. The improvement was made possible by an increase in service charges and bank fees, as well as a $128,000 benefit from the cash surrender value of a bank owned life insurance account. During this same time, non-interest expenses decreased, in part because the bank was finally able to recognize some synergies from its merger with Regal Bancorp.

New Jersey Market Analysis

Because SR Bancorp has all of its offices in the state of New Jersey, the bank is largely at the mercy of the state’s economy. Job growth and economic expansion in the Garden State would be good news for SRBK as well as other local banks, but SR Bancorp is unique in that it is the only publicly traded bank that I could find that operates solely within the state of New Jersey.

The good news for SRBK is that most projections for the state are at least in line with national expectations. GDP in New Jersey is expected to grow by 1.5% in 2025. The state is leading the Northeast in population growth. The number of people living in New Jersey increased by 1.3% in 2024. NJ Labor Commissioner Robert Asaro-Angelo said in January that the state has “more jobs and employers than at any other time in the history of the state.” Nonetheless, unemployment is expected to tick slightly higher this year, peaking around 4.9% compared to 4.6% at the end of 2024.

Because so much of the bank’s loan portfolio is dependent on the housing market and mortgage origination, it is also important to check on expectations for that sector of the state economy. According to Zillow, home sales remain strong in New Jersey, with property values increasing but not as sharply as they have in previous years. The median time on the market for a house is just 20 days. Strong demographics, job growth, and limited supply are all supporting the housing market in the face of elevated interest rates. While none of these facts look like huge green flags for SRBK, there are also no major warning signs at this time.

Risks to Consider

I see three significant risks to consider before investing in SR Bancorp. The first is the chance that we will see a significant slowdown in the overall US economy later this year. Depending on who should be considered the best source, the odds of a recession range from 37%, according to the IMF, to 60%, if JPMorgan is to be believed. If a recession does take place, its length and its severity would impact profit opportunities for all banks in the US, but smaller banks like SR Bancorp could be more severely impacted.

The second risk to consider is the bank’s limited geographical footprint in New Jersey. While the projections mentioned earlier did not seem to show any significant concerns at the current time, there are added risks to investing in a company that is so dependent on one relatively small section of the country. A downturn in employment trends or local political instability could affect SRBK’s bottom line and limit future growth opportunities.

The final risk is the company’s relatively short track record as a publicly traded company. Normally, when I research a stock, I like to see at least three years of financial results, but those do not exist with SRBK. Parsing out the full impact of the company’s addition of Regal Bancorp or comparing like-for-like items in the last annual report are not as simple as they would be if the bank had more years of existence under its belt. The fact that the bank does not seem to update its own Investor Relations website does not help my confidence level.

The Bottom Line

Based on the information that I have been able to gather here, I am holding off on recommending an investment in SRBK for the time being. Investors who have been owners of SR Bancorp over the last year should be thankful for their gains. I think it is safe to assume that the next 12 months may not be as kind to the stock. The risks for the company seem to be at least equal to the potential rewards.

I do think the bank has some value as a potential buyout candidate as the only publicly traded bank to operate exclusively in the state of New Jersey, but when that could happen is anyone's guess at this point. Recent concerns about tariffs and trade wars have made the outlook for most bank mergers and acquisitions exceedingly cloudy. Investors who are looking for another option to invest in a community bank in the Northeast may want to read my recent analysis of Northeast Community Bancorp (NECB), which has increased in value by 11.3% since I rated it as a buy on April 14.

data.title: Verisk Analytics: Benefiting From A Premium Increase But Overvalued
data.description:
I assigned a ‘SELL’ rating to Verisk Analytics (NASDAQ:VRSK) in August 2024, arguing the combination of high quality and a hefty stock valuation. Verisk Analytics has delivered a strong quarterly result in both underwriting and claims segments. I continue to believe the stock price is overvalued and reiterate a ‘Sell’ rating with a fair value of $235 per share.

Verisk Analytics reported its Q1 FY25 result on May 7th, delivering a strong result, beating the market expectations. As illustrated in the chart below, the company delivered 7.9% organic revenue growth in Q1, with 7.2% growth in underwriting & rating and 9.6% in claims business.

The strong financial results have been driven by several factors:

  • During the earnings call, the management highlighted the tailwind from premium increases due to elevated levels of business risks. As Verisk Analytics services the overall insurance industry, a higher premium environment could accelerate Verisk Analytics’s business growth. Considering the increasing uncertainties in tariffs and geopolitical tensions, I anticipate the risk level will remain elevated in the near future.
  • The company’s focus on data, analytics and insights could enable their customers to apply AI to the underwriting and claim processes. The data analytic functionality could help insurance companies reduce costs and mitigate their own operation risks, in my view, as automation is an effective way to reduce human errors during underwriting and claim processes.
  • Verisk Analytics has been launching new databases to expand its business scopes. For instance, they launched Cyber Data Exchange in October 2019, which has achieved significant success among insurance customers. During the call, the management highlighted the company launched Regulatory Data Exchange (RDX) to regulators this April, which could enable regulators to review all regulatory data points. I think these new database launches could enhance Verisk Analytics’s leading position in the data analytics market.
  • Lastly, Verisk Analytics has a diversified submarket, as illustrated in the chart below. The company has expanded into some high-growth areas such as extreme event, anti-fraud as well as specialty business solutions. These new growth areas could accelerate the overall business growth and leverage the company’s existing infrastructure, in my opinion.

Verisk Analytics is guiding for 6%-8% organic revenue growth and around 8.5% growth in adjusted EBITDA, as detailed in the table below:

I anticipate Verisk Analytics will deliver 7% organic revenue growth over the next few years, aligned with the average growth rate of the past four years. As the majority of its business model is subscription-based, the revenue streaming is very recurring in nature. I think the overall premium environment in the insurance market could influence Verisk Analytics’s overall growth. As discussed above, I anticipate the overall insurance premium will continue to move up due to increasing levels of business risks. I anticipate the company will allocate 3% of revenue towards M&A, adding 60bps to the topline growth. I forecast the operating margin will be expanded by 30bps annually, driven by 10bps from pricing increase and 20bps from operating leverage of SG&A expenses. The total operating expenses are calculated to grow by 6.8% annually.

DCF summary:

FCFF calculation:

The WACC is calculated to be 9.4% assuming: risk-free 4.2%; beta 0.94; equity risk premium 6%; cost of debt 5%; equity $38 billion; debt $2.8 billion; tax rate 24%. I set the terminal growth rate to 5%. Discounting all the future FCFF and adjusting net cash, the fair value is calculated to be $235 per share, according to my DCF. The current stock price is trading at more than 35x fwd. FCF, which is a quite high multiple in my view.

Upside Risk

As I assign a ‘SELL’ rating, I am considering the potential upside catalyst for the stock price. Verisk Analytics is a US-centric business, and the potential corporate tax cut could help the company pay less taxes and increase net profits. Currently, the company is paying a 24% tax rate, a typical rate for a US-centric company.

In addition, due to the increasing global risks, insurance premiums could grow beyond the current expectations. In that case, Verisk Analytics’s organic revenue growth could accelerate, which could support an even higher stock multiple.

Conclusion

I trust Verisk Analytics is a high-quality growth company with structural tailwinds. However, the stock is overvalued as per my estimates; therefore, I reiterate a ‘Sell’ rating with a fair value of $235 per share.

data.title: Main Street Capital: A Good Choice To Navigate Concerns Of The BDC Sector
data.description:
Overview

I was lucky enough to initiate my starting position in Main Street Capital (NYSE:MAIN) during the pandemic when shares were trading around $30 per share. Unfortunately, I didn't get the chance to accumulate the position size I wanted before shares took off above the $40 per share mark. At the time, I thought to myself, "I'll just wait for a pullback to add more", but that pullback never came. Over the last five years, MAIN's price has increased so much that the Business Development Company now trades at one of the largest premiums to NAV valuations in the sector.

Despite all of the uncertainty in the market causing indexes to decline, I was a bit relieved to see the price start to come down from its highs over the last month. When I previously covered MAIN, I issued a hold rating due to the expensive valuation. I thought that I'd finally get the chance to continue building my position up. We can see that MAIN's price has declined by about 11.5% over the last three months. However, I am not fully convinced that this is enough of a decline to justify accumulating shares yet. Even with this decline, MAIN still trades at a massive premium to NAV valuation.

I like the underlying fundamentals of MAIN, and it's arguably one of the highest quality choices in the BDC sector at the moment. MAIN recently reported their Q1 earnings and the results continue to be solid, indicating underlying consistency in management's ability to source deals and generate income. I love the fact that MAIN offers a higher starting dividend yield, monthly payouts, and a history of frequent supplemental payments. However, there are some macro concerns that have me hesitant about adding capital to the BDC sector entirely. So let's start by looking at the current valuation and risk profile.

Current Risks Indicate The Golden Era Is Over

Since MAIN is a business development company, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying assets. Looking back over the last decade below, we can see that MAIN has almost exclusively traded in the premium to NAV territory, with the exception of the dip in 2020. MAIN currently trades at a large premium to NAV of 68.5%. The current premium is still above the three-year average premium to NAV of approximately 57.6%.

I have a feeling that we'll start to see the premium valuation come down with many of the higher quality BDCs. This includes large players in the sector like Ares Capital (ARCC), Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL), and Blue Owl Capital (OBDC), all of which are priced at a premium to NAV valuation. Many of these BDCs have some sort of floating rate debt investment exposure in some capacity. Although this has been great for generating a superior level of income from borrowers, it has created an environment that is more difficult to source deals.

Data suggests that there is a growing demand among retail investors in the private credit space. Therefore, there are a growing number of BDCs being launched and a lot more hands chasing a fewer number of deals. Higher interest rates ultimately make it less attractive for businesses to take on debt on a floating rate structure. Higher rates directly translate to higher interest expenses that these businesses have to pay on their loans, which negatively impacts operating margins and profitability.

This has me concerned that many BDCs will begin to lower their underwriting quality and criteria in an effort to source deals. BDCs may begin to loosen the standards and this may lead to portfolios getting exposed to less favorable borrowers. After all, these BDCs need to keep shareholders happy and always show positive new investment activity. Since interest rates remain near their decade high, many BDCs have started to see increases in non-accruals.

Non-accruals represent the rate of borrowers that are materially underperforming and cannot keep with their required debt maintenance. As a result, these non-accrual borrowers reduce a portfolio's quality and can reduce net investment income levels. MAIN's non-accrual rate has consistently increased over the last year, reinforcing this trend. When I last covered MAIN, non-accruals amounted to 1.4% of fair portfolio value for Q4 of 2024. The non-accrual rate for Q3 of 2024 was a bit lower at 1.2% of fair value.

The latest earnings call confirms that non-accruals have now increased to 1.7% of fair portfolio value and 4.5% of cost. Additionally, MAIN experienced net realized losses of $29.5M for the quarter, attributed to exits and restructures of underperforming investments.

The decrease from the fourth quarter was primarily driven by the impact of an increase in investments on non-accrual status, a decrease in net investment activity and a decrease in interest rates on our floating rate debt investments, primarily resulting from decreases in benchmark index rates. - Ryan Nelson, Chief Financial Officer

We've also seen the non-accrual rates increase for many other peers in the BDC space. This is earnings season for many BDCs, so I've been actively reviewing a lot of peers as the reports are issued. Here are some peers that have seen their non-accruals rise:

  • Gladstone Capital (GLAD): non-accruals increased to 7.5% of cost and 4.3% of fair value.
  • Blue Owl Capital (OBDC): non-accruals increased to 1.4% of cost and 0.8% of fair value.
  • Hercules Capital (HTGC): non-accruals increased to 1.8% of cost and 0.5% of fair value.

The Fed decided to keep interest rates unchanged during their latest meeting, citing higher than anticipated inflationary data and a rising unemployment rate. Inflation did slightly decrease to 2.3% for May, but levels still remain higher than the Fed's targeted 2%. Additionally, markets are still trying to determine what sort of ripple effects President Trump's tariff proposals will have once consumers start to feel the weight of increased prices. Therefore, I don't actually think that we'll see any sizeable cuts for 2025.

Therefore, I anticipate that it will become increasingly difficult for many BDCs to be able to originate attractive deals without compromising on some underwriting criteria. Thankfully, MAIN's portfolio is quite diverse and has several operating segments that position it to be better suited to navigate challenging conditions.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest portfolio breakdown, MAIN has total assets of $5.2B that is spread across a range of industries and segments. MAIN breaks its portfolio down into four focus areas, with lower middle market investments making up the largest bulk.

  1. Lower Middle Market: 52%
  2. Private Loans: 38%
  3. Middle Market: 3%
  4. Other Investments: 7%

The lower middle market and private loan portions consists of 189 different portfolio companies at a weighted average yield of 12%. Looking at the pie chart below, we can see that MAIN's portfolio is highly diverse, with Machinery making up the largest bulk of the fund at 9%. This is followed by exposure to Internet Software and Commercial Services investments, accounting for 7% and 6% of assets respectively. MAIN is so large that no specific industry accounts for more than 10% of assets and no individual portfolio company accounts for more than 3.2%.

Over 99% of MAIN's lower middle market portfolio is structured on a first lien senior secured basis. First lien debt sits at the top of the capital structure and has the highest priority for repayment. This significantly reduces the risk that MAIN loses all invested capital when an investment doesn't go as planned. If a borrower is materially underperforming and has to liquidate assets, MAIN would be entitled for repayment before other forms of debt. This portion of MAIN's portfolio is a bit unique because 74% of the investments are structured as fixed rate investments. It's more typical to see a BDC put a larger emphasis on floating rate investments.

The private loan portion of the portfolio is where MAIN has a primary focus on variable rate investments, as 97% of these investments are floating rate. Therefore, these two segments of the portfolio work in tandem with one another. The lower middle market portfolio is a bit more steady and resilient to higher interest rates, but may not be able to generate higher income during a higher rate environment. Conversely, the private loan portion may be able to better capitalize on this period of higher interest rates, but may introduce more risk to the portfolio.

Q1 Earnings

MAIN reported its Q1 earnings in early May and I believe the results were solid. Most notably, net investment income amounted to $1.07 per share, beating estimates by $0.05. We can see that total investment income amounted to $137M, representing a slight 4% year-over-year growth. However, interest expenses and G&A expenses both increased, which ultimately led the distributable net investment income to slightly decrease from last year. Additionally, MAIN experienced net realized losses of $29.5M during the quarter.

Despite these challenges, MAIN was still able to see a net increase in net assets of $116M. The distributable net investment income margin has slightly decreased year over year to 69.2%, but this is understandable because there were some small interest rate cuts at the end of 2024. On a more positive note, the DNII margin has increased from the prior quarter.

MAIN has been able to maintain a positive growth streak because of management's excellent track record of allocating capital towards new investments. MAIN was able to allocate $86M towards new investment activity within the lower middle market portion of their portfolio. $62M worth of this total went towards two new portfolio companies. Even after sales and repayments during the quarter, this segment still had a net increase in the portfolio of $57M.

There was also another $138M invested in the private loan segment, which saw net growth of $26M after sales and repayments. This growth allows MAIN to keep its NAV trending upward. Here are the quarter-end NAV amounts:

  • Q1 2024: $29.54 per share
  • Q2 2024: $29.80 per share
  • Q3 2024: $30.57 per share
  • Q4 2024: $31.65 per share
  • Q1 2025: $32.03 per share

An increasing NAV is one of the clearest indications that a BDC is in great shape. This is only further reinforced by MAIN's solid liquidity profile. There is currently $109.1M in cash and equivalents available, accompanied by another $1.1B in credit facility available. Although MAIN's total debt has slightly increased to $2.2B, they are still well positioned to navigate headwinds.

Dividend Support

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.255 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 5.7%, not including distributions. If we multiply this monthly payout amount by three, we get an estimated quarterly-based payout of $0.765 per share. As previously mentioned, net investment income for the quarter amounted to $1.07 per share. This indicates that earnings support the distribution at a very strong coverage rate of nearly 140%. Coverage has been so strong that MAIN also announced a $0.30 per share supplemental distribution that will be payable in June.

MAIN has the most impressive track record of dividend payouts over the last decade. Although the starting yield is already high, MAIN has managed to reward shareholders with a combination of base raises and ongoing supplementals when conditions can support them. For instance, the dividend has increased at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 3.81% over the last five years. As the private loan portion of the portfolio continues to generate elevated income levels due to its floating rate structure, MAIN has continued to pay out supplementals over the last few years. As mentioned, I don't think there will be any sizable interest rate cuts over the course of 2025, so I do anticipate these supplementals to continue.

However, it's worth noting that the distributions received from MAIN are typically classified as ordinary dividends, since dividends are fueled by net investment income. Therefore, investors should consider the tax implications of maintaining a position. Based on your current tax bracket, amount of capital invested, and desired income level, it may be best to utilize MAIN within a tax-advantaged account to offset any potential tax burdens.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my hold rating on MAIN, as the premium valuation still trends above the average. The BDC sector has experienced a growing interest from retail investors, leading to increased competition in the sector to find attractive deals. I worry about that BDCs will start to see rising non-accrual levels that will cause valuations to decline. However, MAIN is arguably best suited to navigate these challenging conditions as interest rates remain elevated. The distribution is well-supported by earnings, and management continues to allocate capital towards new investment activity that can lead to NAV growth. I will maintain my position and DRIP any distributions for now.

data.title: Tracking Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway Portfolio - Q1 2025 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to Berkshire Hathaway’s 13F stock portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on Warren Buffett’s regulatory 13F Form filed on 05/15/2025. Please visit our Tracking 10 Years Of Berkshire Hathaway's Investment Portfolio article series for an idea on how his holdings have progressed over the years and our previous update for the moves during Q4 2024.

During Q1 2025, Berkshire Hathaway’s (NYSE:BRK.A) (NYSE:BRK.B) 13F stock portfolio value decreased from ~$267B to ~$259B. The top five positions account for ~70% of the portfolio: Apple Inc., American Express, Coca Cola, Bank of America, and Chevron. There are 33 individual stock positions, many of which are small compared to the overall size of the portfolio.

Warren Buffett’s writings (pdfs) are a treasure trove of information and are a very good source for anyone starting out on individual investing. So are the classics The Intelligent Investor and Security Analysis.

Note 1: Berkshire Hathaway BYD Company (OTCPK:BYDDY) stake fell below the 5% ownership threshold during Q2 2024. Their cost-basis is ~8 HKD per share. This is compared to the current traded price in Hong Kong of ~420 HKD. Berkshire’s ownership was down from ~20% as of Q3 2022.

Note 3: It was disclosed in August 2020 that Berkshire had built ~5% stakes in five Japanese trading businesses: ITOCHU, Marubeni, Mitsubishi, Mitsui, and Sumitomo. The stakes increased to over 10% each as of Q1 2025. The 2021 AR had the following regarding the initial purchases in three of them: 5.6% ownership stake in ITOCHU Corporation (OTCPK:ITOCF) at a cost-basis of $23.52, 5.5% ownership stake in Mitsubishi Corporation (OTCPK:MSBHF) at a cost-basis of $8.57, and 5.7% ownership stake in Mitsui & Company (OTCPK:MITSF) at a cost-basis of $8.65. ITOCF, MSBHF, and MITSF currently trade at ~$52, ~$20, and ~$20 respectively. Mitsubishi had a 3-for-1 stock split in December 2023 and Mitsui had a 2-for-1 stock split in June 2024. The cost-basis are adjusted to reflect this.

Stake Disposals:

Citigroup Inc. (C): The 0.39% of the portfolio stake in Citigroup was purchased in Q1 2022 at prices between ~$53 and ~$68. There was a ~75% reduction during the last quarter at prices between $60.35 and $72. The disposal this quarter was at prices between ~$66 and ~$85. The stock currently trades at ~$76.

Nu Holdings (NU): NU had an IPO during December 2021. Shares started trading at ~$10 and currently goes for $13.13. Berkshire’s ~$1B stake went back to a funding round in early 2021. Q3 2024 saw a ~20% selling at prices between $10.71 and $15.14. That was followed by a ~50% reduction during the last quarter at prices between $10.24 and $15.89. The elimination this quarter was at prices between ~$10 and ~$14.

Stake Increases:

Occidental Petroleum (OXY): Berkshire made a $10B investment in OXY in April 2019 through 100,000 preferred shares that has a liquidation value of $100,000 per share. Those shares pay 8% dividend, and the transaction came with warrants to purchase 83.86M shares at $59.62. In Q1 2022, Berkshire purchased ~136.4M shares at prices between ~$31 and ~$59 per share. That was followed with a ~22M share net-increase in Q2 2022 at prices between ~$57 and ~$60. The next quarter also saw a ~23% stake increase at prices between ~$57 and ~$75. The three quarters through Q2 2024 also saw a ~15% stake increase. The stock currently trades at $43.37. There was a minor ~3% stake increase during the last quarter and a marginal increase this quarter.

Note 1: Including warrants, Berkshire owns ~36% of the business (~350M shares). Those warrants expire one year after the preferred shares are fully redeemed. The tenth-year anniversary of preferred issuance from when they can be redeemed at 105% of PAR starts in 2029. 15% of the $10B in preferred was redeemed in 2023 at 110% of PAR (terms of the preferred required OXY to redeem a portion if they returned more capital to shareholders than the threshold specified).

Note 2: The publicly traded warrants under the symbol OXY.WS were initially distributed in June 2020 and allow holders to purchase one share of OXY common stock at $22 per share until August 3, 2027. A temporary offer allowed holders to exercise the warrants at a reduced price of $21.30 until March 31, 2025.

VeriSign Inc. (VRSN): VRSN was first purchased in Q4 2012 at prices between $34 and $49.50. The position was more than doubled in Q1 2013 at prices between $38 and $48. The buying continued till Q2 2014 at prices up to $63. The stock currently trades at ~$282 and the position is at 1.30% of the portfolio (~14% of the business). There was a minor ~4% stake increase during the last quarter and a marginal increase this quarter.

Sirius XM Holdings (SIRI): The stake came about as a result of a redistribution transaction by Liberty Media Group. The position was increased by ~315% during Q4 2023 as SIRI traded between ~$41 and ~$57. Q2 2024 saw another ~260% stake increase at prices between ~$25 and ~$38. That was followed by a ~12% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between $20.37 and $28.44. It currently trades at $22.36. There was a minor ~2% further increase this quarter.

Note: in December 2023, Liberty Media announced a plan to simplify the structure - Liberty SiriusXM to combine with Sirius XM to form a new entity. The consolidation ratios were 0.8375 of new SIRI for each LSXMK/LSXMA and 0.1 of new SIRI for each SIRI held. The transaction closed during the last quarter. The prices quoted above are adjusted based on the conversion ratio.

Constellation Brands (STZ): STZ is a 0.85% of the portfolio position purchased during the last quarter at prices between ~$218 and ~$255. This quarter saw the position increased by ~114% at prices between ~$161 and ~$229. The stock currently trades at ~$191.

Note: they have a ~6.6% ownership stake in the business.

Domino’s Pizza (DPZ): DPZ is a 0.47% of the portfolio position purchased during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$402 and ~$504. There was a ~87% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$407 and ~$475. This quarter saw a ~10% further increase at prices between ~$397 and ~$501. The stock currently trades at ~$486.

Note: they have a ~7.7% ownership stake in the business.

Pool Corp (POOL): The very small 0.18% of the portfolio POOL stake was established during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$294 and ~$372. The last quarter saw a ~48% stake increase at prices between ~$339 and ~$390. That was followed by a ~145% stake increase this quarter at prices between ~$312 and ~$375. The stock is now at ~$314.

HEICO Corporation (HEI): The 0.09% HEI stake was purchased during Q2 2024 at prices between ~$188 and ~$229. There was a ~11% stake increase this quarter at prices between ~$217 and ~$273. The stock is now at ~$275.

Stake Decreases:

Bank of America (BAC): Berkshire established this large (top five) ~10% of the portfolio position through the exercise of Bank of America warrants. The warrants had a strike price of $7.14. The cost to exercise was $5B, and it was funded using the $5B in 6% preferred stock they held. There was a ~30% stake increase in Q3 2018 at prices between $27.75 and $31.80. Q3 2024 saw a ~23% selling at prices between $36.41 and $43.84. That was followed by a ~15% reduction during the last quarter at prices between $39 and $47.51. This quarter also saw a ~7% trimming at prices between ~$39 and ~$48. The stock currently trades at $44.38.

Note: Berkshire’s overall cost-basis is ~$14 and ownership stake is at ~8.3%.

DaVita Inc. (DVA): DVA is a ~2% of the portfolio position that was aggressively built over several quarters in the 2012-13 timeframe at prices between $30 and $49. The stock currently trades at ~$144 compared to Berkshire’s overall cost-basis of ~$45 per share. Q3 2020 saw a ~5% (2M shares) selling at $88 per share. This quarter also saw a ~3% trimming.

Note: Berkshire’s ownership stake in DaVita is ~42%.

Liberty Media Corp (FWONK) (LLYVK) (LLYVA): These tracking stocks were first acquired as a result of Liberty Media’s recapitalization in April 2016. Shareholders received 1 share of Liberty SiriusXM Group, 0.25 shares of Liberty Media Group and 0.1 shares of Liberty Braves Group for each share held. Berkshire held 30M shares of Liberty Media, for which they received the same amount of Liberty SiriusXM Group shares. Since then, there have been several transactions, redistributions, etc. Last August saw another reclassification, whereby Liberty SiriusXM common stock was reclassified, and shareholders received 0.25 shares of Liberty Live for each share of Liberty SiriusXM. Also, Liberty Formula One was reclassified, and shareholders received 0.0428 shares of Liberty Live for each share of Liberty Formula One. The net share count increased by ~23% as a result of these transactions. The Liberty SiriusXM stake saw a ~55% stake increase during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$28.50 and ~$31.25. FWONK and LLYVK currently trade at ~$97 and ~$78 respectively. There was a minor ~15% overall reduction this quarter.

Capital One Financial (COF): The 0.50% of the portfolio stake in COF was purchased during Q1 2023 at prices between ~$90 and ~$122. There was a ~25% stake increase in the next quarter at prices between ~$86 and ~$114. Q2 2024 saw a ~21% reduction at prices between ~$133 and ~$148. The next quarter also saw a ~7% trimming at prices between ~$131 and ~$153. That was followed by a ~18% selling during the last quarter at prices between ~$145 and ~$192. The stock currently trades at ~$198. There was a ~4% trimming this quarter.

T-Mobile US (TMUS): TMUS is a 0.40% of the portfolio stake purchased in Q3 2020 at prices between ~$104 and ~$119 and more than doubled in the next quarter at prices between ~$110 and ~$135. There was a ~11% trimming during Q2 2024 at prices between ~$159 and ~$181. That was followed by a ~7% trimming during the last quarter at prices between ~$205 and ~$247. This quarter also saw a ~11% reduction at prices between ~$208 and ~$277. The stock currently trades at ~$240.

Charter Communications (CHTR): CHTR is a 0.28% of the portfolio position. It was established during the last three quarters of 2014 at prices between $118 and $170. In Q2 2015, the position was increased by ~42% at prices between $168 and $193 and that was followed with another ~21% increase the following quarter at prices between $167 and $195. The six quarters thru Q4 2018 had seen a combined ~25% selling at prices between $250 and $395 and that was followed with a ~20% reduction in Q1 2019 at prices between $285 and $366. H2 2021 saw another ~25% reduction at prices between ~$605 and ~$821. That was followed by similar selling during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$291 and ~$383. The last quarter also saw another ~30% reduction at prices between ~$315 and ~$408. The stock currently trades at ~$420 compared to Berkshire’s cost-basis of ~$178. There was marginal trimming this quarter.

Kept Steady:

Apple Inc. (AAPL): AAPL is currently the largest 13F portfolio stake by far at ~26%. It was built between Q1 2016 and Q1 2018 at a cost-basis of ~$35 per share. The stake was decreased by 13% during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$169 and ~$195. The next quarter saw another ~49% selling at prices between ~$165 and ~$216. That was followed by a ~25% reduction during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$207 and ~$234. The stock currently trades at ~$212.

American Express (AXP) and Coca-Cola (KO): These two very large stakes were kept steady during the last ~10 years. Buffett has said these positions will be held permanently. Berkshire’s cost-basis on AXP and KO are at around $8.49 and $3.25 respectively and the ownership stakes are at ~20% and ~9.2% respectively.

Chevron Corp (CVX): CVX is a large (top five) 7.67% of the portfolio position purchased in Q3 2020 at prices between ~$72 and ~$91. Q1 2021 saw a ~50% selling at prices between ~$85 and ~$112 while in Q3 2021 there was a ~25% stake increase at prices between ~$94 and ~$106. That was followed with another one-third increase in the next quarter at prices between ~$102 and ~$119. Q1 2022 saw a whopping ~315% stake increase at prices between ~$119 and ~$171. The three quarters through Q3 2023 saw a one third selling at prices between ~$150 and ~$188. There was a ~14% stake increase during Q4 2023 at prices between ~$142 and ~$169. The stock currently trades at ~$142. H1 2024 saw a ~6% trimming.

Note 1: Berkshire has a ~5.5% ownership stake in the business.

Note 2: Berkshire avoided disclosing this stake in the Q3 2020 13F filing by making use of the section 13(f) Confidential Treatment Requests. An amendment filed later disclosed the activity.

Moody's Inc. (MCO): MCO is a 4.44% of the 13F portfolio stake. It is a very long-term position and Buffett’s cost-basis is $10.05. The stock currently trades at ~$487. Berkshire controls ~13% of the business.

Kraft Heinz Co. (KHC): KHC is currently a fairly large position at 3.83% of the portfolio. Kraft Heinz started trading in July 2015 with Berkshire owning just over 325M shares (~26.5% of the business). The stake came about because of two transactions with 3G capital as partner: a ~$4B net investment in 2013 for half of Heinz and a ~$5B investment for the acquisition of Kraft Foods Group in early 2015. Berkshire’s cost-basis on KHC is ~$30 per share compared to the current price of $27.49.

Chubb Limited (CB): CB is a 3.16% of the portfolio position purchased during H2 2023 at prices between ~$188 and ~$230. The next quarter saw a ~30% stake increase at prices between ~$225 and ~$259. The stock currently trades at ~$292.

Note: Berkshire avoided disclosing this stake in the Q3 2023 13F filing by making use of the section 13(f) Confidential Treatment Requests. They have a ~6.7% ownership stake in the business.

Kroger Company (KR): KR is a 1.31% of the portfolio position established in Q4 2019 at prices between $24 and $29. The five quarters through Q3 2021 had seen a ~225% stake increase at prices between ~$30 and ~$40. The three quarters through Q3 2022 saw a ~20% selling at prices between ~$44 and ~$52. The stock currently trades at $67.60.

Note: Berkshire’s ownership stake in Kroger is ~7%.

Amazon.com (AMZN): AMZN is a 0.74% of the portfolio stake established during Q1 2019 at prices between ~$75 and ~$91. The stake was increased by ~11% in the next quarter at prices between ~$85 and ~$98. The stock currently trades at ~$205. Q3 2023 saw a ~5% trimming.

Aon plc (AON): AON is a 0.63% of the portfolio position established during Q1 2021 at prices between ~$202 and ~$234. The next quarter saw a ~7% increase at prices between ~$230 and ~$259. The stock currently trades at ~$359.

Ally Financial (ALLY): The 0.41% ALLY stake saw a whopping ~235% increase during Q2 2022 at prices between ~$32 and ~$53. The stock currently trades at $35.75.

Note: Berkshire has a ~9.6% ownership stake in Ally Financial.

Louisiana-Pacific (LPX): The 0.20% LPX position was established during Q4 2022 at prices between ~$48.40 and ~$65. There was a ~22% stake increase in the next quarter at prices between ~$51 and ~$66. The stock is now at $93.44. There was a ~6% trimming during Q1 2024 and that was followed by a ~10% trimming during the next quarter. The last quarter also saw a ~5% trimming.

Note: Berkshire has a ~8% ownership stake in the business.

Atlanta Braves (BATRK), Diageo plc (DEO), Jefferies Financial Group (JEF), Liberty LiLAC Group (LILA), Lennar Corp (LEN.B), MasterCard Inc. (MA), NVR Inc. (NVR), and Visa Inc. (V): These positions were kept steady this quarter.
